speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 25, 1996. The interview is with Alfred Traum. Being a refugee and escaping from the Nazis, how has that impacted your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, see, this is a tough question. Because I think the impact, it's always with you. It doesn't mean to say that all day long, you're thinking about your experiences, or you're thinking back of your life as an early childhood. But it doesn't take much for some experience, some, some words, some sound, some smell, or anything like that to bring it back to you. And you're always carrying it around with you. It's baggage that you've got that you can't get rid of. And in one respect, you don't want to get rid of it. It's impacted me in the fact that I, I feel-- a kind of obligation that I-- I have [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to my parents, that I have to continue, continue [SOBS] on being Jewish. I, I know that there's a lot of, you know, mixed marriages. And you know, everybody does what they want to do. But I kind of have a feeling that in, in our case, or in my case, that you, you, you have an obligation that you have to live up to.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I think it's impacted me in this respect, that perhaps might have carried over to my, my daughter, that, you know, perhaps I was demanding, or she felt that it was-- she had to live up to our expectations. What is your daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yael. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, you know, fortunately, she's happily married, and everything is great there. But I, I don't know if, if she felt that pressure. And I wouldn't be one bit surprised if she did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I think it's, it's had its varying impacts on our lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I've had Michael and Johnathan. Michael is the reason, as I mentioned, we came here. And he's in a, in a group home. He's autistic. And Johnathan, who is younger, also has-- is brain injured. He's-- he's a lovable young guy. But obviously, he has lots of problems. And he's working in a sheltered workshop.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know, we are fortunate, since we do have boys that have problems that we're living in an environment, in a country, which is more enlightened than most, in being able to provide services in these areas. So you know, we're very much involved with those organizations. Do you have grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: One grandchild, son, Isaac. He's the apple of everyone's eye. He's 17 months old. He's a beautiful little boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a message that you would like to say today to your children and grandchild?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm [SOBBING] hoping that-- the story in itself will bear out the message.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I kind of feel like I've said it all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, good. OK, thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. Yeah, I'd like to introduce now some of my family members. My wife, Josie. She's the one I talked about. I met on the ship, many, many years ago. And we've been married for 38 years. And we're very happily married. And also here is Yael. Yael was born in Israel. And of course, she lives here now and also married, with a wonderful grandson. And maybe, maybe you want to say something, please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as you mentioned before, that we've been married for 38 years. And I truly feel that I've been very fortunate, in having had someone like you. And I feel that both of us being child survivors has brought a lot in common to our relationship and something very important and has certainly helped us develop our relationship. And I'm very grateful for that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you don't have to be grateful. [LAUGHS] This is something we feel for each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think it's a common bond. What about you? How has it impacted you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess first, I just want to add-- I, I know you said this on your tape. But I'm one of three children. I'm the middle child. And I think both my parents being survivors has, has shaped our family. It really came along with the identity of being Jewish. It was sort of in one sentence-- I'm Jewish, and my family are survivors. So I can't imagine what life would have been without having that in the family. I want to thank you both for doing this. I think that that was very courageous. I feel very [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] happy-- not happy-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very fortunate to be able to have this document in the family. It's a document that I will cherish and pass down to my children. And I know that it will always be passed down. But I guess the other thing that I'd like to add is while I-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] while I think of our identity as a family as survivors and often think of the tragedy that you both went through and cannot even imagine going through that myself, I'm more amazed at how much laughter there is in our family. And that's what really stands out, to me. Despite a lot of difficult times in the past and difficult times that we seem to go through over the years, when I think about the amount of humor in the family, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that, that stands out. And I think I'm more grateful for the way you've come through your difficult time and gone on and persevered in life. And that's what I've learned more from than anything else. So thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of myself, Alfred Traum, with my sister Ruth. And I'm on my tricycle. Always had a love for bicycles, and this was my first one. I must have been about 5 years old. And this is in our backyard in Vienna. OK and this-- this is me, Freddie Traum. I must have been about 7, 8, about 1937, '38, in our backyard, where there were some cow sheds. And I used to spend quite a bit of time there, working or helping out. I thought I was working. And you can see me. I've got these typical leather trousers on there. And I felt very much at home in that environment there. Bicycles have always been an enjoyable thing for me. There, this is me, Freddie Traum, on the bike and my cousin, Yossi Traum, standing next to me. Unfortunately, he did not survive the war. He stayed behind in Vienna. Yeah, this is a picture of a school-leaving certificate. This was from my last school in Vienna, the one which was a school for Jews. And it was quite a long way from home. The signature, which is not readable here, was a Professor Schwarzbard, which was an eminent professor in the university before he was downgraded to teach 9-year-olds. But his loss was our gain. This was given to, to me, just before I left school, for leaving for England. Professor Schwarzbard, whose signature is seen here, his son was about 5 years old, also joined on the Kindertransport. And I remember the father coming up to me and asking me to look out for him on the trip. So he actually set with-- sat with me the whole period. I never saw him again, after we got to England. My father was an amateur photographer and did his own processing and printing. And this, he took of himself in our backyard, on the day when we were leaving for England. So this was on the 20th of June, 1939. This is a family picture, obviously, not in a very happy mood. We were just on our way, leaving home for England. On the left is my sister, Ruth Traum. Behind her is my grandmother, Bena Alster. Sitting down, holding my sister's hand, is my mother, Gita Traum. My father is standing behind, leaning over, [SOBBING] clutching me. [SOBBING] The, the words on top say, der abschied, which means the farewell. This is my passport. And it-- you can see the Nazi emblem, at the bottom of my picture. And it says on there, fremdenpass, which also the status on top there says stateless. Because at that point, I didn't have any nationality. And that was a kind of a travel document. Now further inside, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] this, this visa for the United Kingdom was my ticket to life. At the bottom, you see a Harwich embarkation port in, in the UK. And on the left is a exit permit, leaving Austria. So this was a very hard to come by and very important stamp. Here, this is a picture of my newly acquired family. On the left is my sister Ruth, Ruth Traum. In the middle is Charles Griggs, Mr. Charles Griggs, who was my new guardian. And he is holding his son, Charlie Griggs. And to his right is Kitty Griggs. And we're still in touch with both of them, Charlie and Kitty. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This was taken shortly after we arrived in England. And the reason I'm not on the photograph is I was the one that took it. This picture was taken in 1941. I was about 12 years old, and I think I had mentioned, I worked part time on a farm. And somebody introduced me to this man, who obviously had come from Austria and wanted to meet me. And we had a conversation, but I don't recall who he is now. But it was happy time, working on the farm there. This is a picture of my sister, Ruth Traum, on the left and Kitty Griggs on the right. It was taken around about 1945, in the back garden of the house we lived in in London. This is in 1945, about, I was about 15, 16 years old, on my trip to Scotland. Nobody would be complete without donning a kilt and the rest of the gear. So I had my photograph taken. Yeah, this is a picture of when I was in the Israeli army. And the period was about 1949. And I was 19 years old. And I-- don't recall any particular situation there, except that I was assigned to a truck, and I was a rifleman. After serving the Israeli army, I went back and did my service in the British army. So this was stationed in Germany, several tank crews together. I'm there on the extreme left, without the hat on. And we were just on some maneuver and parades. OK, on the, on this picture, standing next to me-- I'm on the extreme right-- next to me is Hughes. And he was a driver. Always wanted to-- me to translate for him. And I think I talked about that, when we ran over the signs. But there were several other. There was a fellow by the name of Bell. He's the third from the right. And everybody had a nickname there. His name was Dinger. Anybody with-- from the north were [INAUDIBLE]. They were, they were a good group. And we spent a lot of time together. And it was quite an enjoyable time. This is a picture of me, Freddie Traum, after graduate-- graduating from the Merchant Marine Academy. And I was ready to look for a ship. So just before I joined my first ship. This is a picture taken on the deck of the Now, this was taken in 1957. On the extreme left is [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Larry Buchsbaum. At that time, he used to go by Buxy. He was a chief radio officer, whose position I took over from him when he left. In the middle was Yakov [NON-ENGLISH]. And I'm on the right, Freddie Traum. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was-- I think I mentioned when it was taken, '57. The became my ship. I was-- I joined it when it was being built in Hamburg. It was part of reparations agreement. And I stayed on it for four years. And at this point, I was chief radio officer. And this is at our marriage. We were married on board the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this ship had a very, very close feeling to it for me. So here I am, Freddie Traum on the left and Josie Traum on my right. And the year was 1958.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my sister in Israel, my sister Ruth Doniger. I think she likes to blend in. Always chooses some kind of background where she almost loses herself in. But this was taken about 10 years ago or so, around about 1986. This is a picture of my daughter Yael on the right and my son Michael on the left and taken in a nursery school. I think the year was 1962 or possibly beginning of '63, just before they came to the United States. It was a Hanukkah before that. Yeah, this is a picture of my daughter's wedding, taken at Brookside Garden. On the left is my wife's mother, Fannie Aizenberg. Next to her is my son, Michael Traum. Then it's Josiane Traum, my wife. Then Yael, now [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yael Traum Futrovsky, next to her is Lee Futrovsky. Then Freddie Traum, myself. And next, standing on my left, is Johnathan, my youngest son. And the year was 1991. This is a picture of myself, Freddie Traum, and my wife, Josie Traum. We were hiking the Grand Canyon, in 1995. Wonderful place to visit.
